messages.content:
What are key objectives of DevOps at Accenture?


to collaborate with customers on the requirements, design, and feedback prior to development


to gather requirements, design, code, test, and deploy the product prior to customer feedback


to deliver services faster, with higher quality, and with built in security


to create a minimally viable product before building the final, polished end product

to ensure continuous integration and continuous delivery of software projects, reducing the time between concept and delivery

to adopt an agile development methodology, allowing for quick and efficient responses to changing requirements and market demands

to automate and streamline processes, reducing the risk of errors and improving efficiency

to encourage collaboration and communication between development and operations teams, ensuring a smooth and seamless product delivery process

to monitor and analyze system performance, identifying areas for improvement and proactively addressing any issues that arise.

From development to testing and deployment, how are DevOps practices best orchestrated?


program management


milestones


phases


pipelines

DevOps practices are best orchestrated through pipelines. A pipeline is a set of automated processes that allow for the seamless integration and testing of software code throughout the development lifecycle. Pipelines typically involve a series of stages that allow code to move seamlessly from development to testing and deployment. 

Each stage of the pipeline involves specific activities designed to test and validate code, including unit tests, integration testing, and user acceptance testing. Additionally, pipelines are often designed with automation and collaboration in mind, allowing developers and operations teams to work together and troubleshoot issues in real-time.

Overall, pipelines are an essential aspect of DevOps practices, helping to ensure that software is delivered quickly and reliably while maintaining a high level of quality and security.

Organizations following Waterfall methodology usually begin with requirements gathering, in which the development team attempts to fully understand the product and all its desired features. This process may take months under the traditional approach.

How is the Agile way of working different?


Agile expects teams to work quickly and gather all the requirements in a shorter duration of time.


Agile requires thoroughly documenting the requirements and getting them approved by the client.


Agile presumes that requirements cannot fully be known up front, because they are always changing.


Agile recommends breaking the work into distinct functional phases and creating timelines for each of them.

Agile is different from the Waterfall methodology in that it presumes that requirements cannot fully be known up front and that they are always changing. Under Agile, development teams work in short iterations or sprints, which typically last from one to four weeks. During each sprint, teams prioritize a subset of requirements and work to deliver a small but valuable piece of functionality.

Agile relies on continuous feedback from the client and stakeholders, with frequent reviews and demo sessions to ensure that the product development is on track. Through these feedback sessions, the product is refined and prioritized based on the client's changing needs.

Rather than putting all the requirements into a single phase of development, Agile recommends breaking the work into distinct functional phases and continually testing and delivering working software throughout the development cycle. Agile teams do not spend months gathering, approving, and documenting all the requirements upfront, but they work in an iterative process of discovery and delivery that allows them to adapt to evolving needs and changing requirements as they emerge.

Which phase(s) of software production are the focus of DevOps?


design, definition, and maintenance


requirements gathering and test


development team management and maintenance


development, testing, and deployment

DevOps focuses on development, testing, and deployment phases of software production. DevOps is a development workflow that emphasizes seamless and collaborative interactions between development and operations teams to deliver software faster, more efficiently, and with greater reliability. 

In the development phase, DevOps practices strive to create a culture of continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) by automating the build and test processes. This helps to ensure that code is always in a deployable state, and new features can be released quickly with minimal risk.

The testing phase in DevOps is also crucial, as it emphasizes continuous testing and automated quality assurance practices. This helps to ensure that any new software updates or features do not adversely impact existing functionality or create new bugs.

Finally, the deployment phase in DevOps focuses on continuous delivery, rapid deployment, and change management practices that allow for seamless and reliable software updates, without causing downtime or affecting end-users.

Overall, by leveraging DevOps practices, organizations can boost productivity, speed up the software development lifecycle, and deliver high-quality software products.

Scrum teams use short iterations to receive quick feedback from customers.

What is the name for this timeboxed iteration?


quarters


sequences


blocks


sprints

The name for the timeboxed iteration in Scrum is called a sprint. Sprints are typically two to four weeks long and allow a Scrum team to focus on a specific set of tasks or objectives. During each sprint, the team plans, designs, develops, tests, and delivers a potentially shippable product increment or feature.

The primary goal of a sprint is to deliver a working software increment or functionality that can be reviewed and validated by customers or stakeholders. Through this mechanism of timeboxed iterations, Scrum teams can receive quick feedback from customers and adjust their development efforts accordingly.

At the end of each sprint, the team holds a sprint review meeting to showcase the completed increment and obtain feedback from the stakeholders. This feedback is then used to refine or reprioritize the product backlog for the next sprint iteration.

Overall, the iterative and timeboxed approach of Scrum enables teams to adapt to changing requirements and deliver value to customers in a faster and more efficient manner.

An Accenture consulting team is tasked with developing a new client operating model and decide to follow the Waterfall approach. The team misses the client deadline because they did not work together and share information. For the next iteration, the team decides to work in a series of sprints, with brief daily meetings to ensure all team members collaborate effectively.

Which Agile approach is described in this example?


Scrum


Kanban


Design Thinking


XP

The Agile approach described in this example is Scrum. Scrum is a popular Agile approach that focuses on delivering working software in short, timeboxed iterations called sprints. 

In Scrum, the development team works collaboratively to deliver a potentially shippable increment of a product or feature at the end of each sprint. The team holds daily stand-up meetings called "daily scrums" to discuss progress, blockers, and any necessary adjustments to the sprint backlog.

By working in sprints and holding daily scrum meetings, Scrum teams can address issues and challenges in real-time, and ensure that all team members collaborate effectively. This promotes a culture of teamwork and helps to deliver value to the client in a more efficient and timely manner. 

Through this approach, Scrum teams can quickly identify the areas that need improvement, incorporate customer feedback, and adjust their development efforts accordingly, resulting in faster delivery of high-quality products or services.

In DevOps, high levels of automation are expected, which increases productivity.

Which fact illustrates this productivity increase?


Products can be built with higher quality and faster speed to market.


Up to 100% of support tickets can be solved without human intervention.


One engineer can look after 10 to 20 servers.


Fewer processes have to be automated.

The following fact illustrates the productivity increase from automation in DevOps:

Products can be built with higher quality and faster speed to market.

The high levels of automation in DevOps can speed up the process of building and delivering software products, while also improving the quality of the software. Automation can help reduce errors and increase efficiency, leading to faster and more reliable delivery of software products. By automating tasks like building, testing, and deployment, developers can spend more time writing valuable code instead of performing manual tasks.

For example, automated testing tools can run tests on a piece of code every time a change is made, allowing developers to catch bugs early on before they become bigger issues. This can result in higher quality software that is delivered faster.

Overall, automation in DevOps can help organizations achieve better collaboration between development and operations, while also delivering software products faster and with higher quality, ultimately improving productivity and efficiency.

How can a DevOps team take advantage of Artificial Intelligence (AI)?


by ensuring developers have the time needed to write and release code


by using AI to collate data from multiple sources and assess existing automation to improve efficiency


by focusing on multiple views into systems, and pushing DevOps issues across the process


by allowing AI to organize data into separate silos for better data aggregation

DevOps teams can take advantage of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the following ways:

1. Using AI for data analysis: DevOps teams can use AI to collate data from multiple sources and assess existing automation to improve efficiency. AI can also be used to analyze logs, detect anomalies, and monitor system performance, identifying the areas for improvement and providing actionable insights to the team.

2. Automating routine tasks: DevOps teams can use AI to automate routine tasks, such as code deployment and testing. This can help speed up the development process and reduce the risk of human error.

3. Predictive analytics: AI can help DevOps teams predict and prevent potential issues, such as system failures or unexpected downtime. Through the use of machine learning algorithms, AI can analyze data and identify patterns that can help forecast future problems.

4. Chatbots and virtual assistants: DevOps teams can use AI-powered chatbots or virtual assistants to provide support to end-users, helping to resolve issues quickly and efficiently.

Overall, by incorporating AI into DevOps practices, teams can streamline processes, identify issues early on, and automate routine tasks, freeing up time to focus on more critical tasks, improving productivity, and delivering software products that meet end-users' needs.

When comparing Waterfall methodology to the Scrum framework, how is the overall Scrum process often described?


as a marathon race


as a cross country race


as a series of sprints


as a relay race

When comparing Waterfall methodology to the Scrum framework, the overall Scrum process is often described as a series of sprints. The Scrum framework follows an Agile development methodology, which emphasizes iterative and incremental delivery of working software. 

Each sprint comprises a set period of time (usually two to four weeks) during which the Scrum team works collaboratively to deliver a potentially shippable product increment. Each sprint begins with a planning meeting, in which team members select a set of prioritized backlog items they will deliver by the end of the sprint. 

During the sprint, the Scrum team works on completing these items in a highly collaborative manner, with daily stand-up meetings to discuss progress, blockers, and collaboration. At the end of the sprint, the team conducts a sprint review meeting, where the completed work is reviewed by the stakeholders, and feedback is obtained. 

The Scrum process is iterative, with each sprint building on the work completed in the previous sprint. Like a series of sprints in a race, each sprint aims to deliver incremental value to the customer and achieve maximum efficiency. Over time, the Scrum team iteratively and incrementally delivers a high-quality, valuable product.
